parts.heading: Introduction
parts.text: The As-Is area of the SEPA Payment Processing (SPP) process documents the current state of the bank's outbound euro credit transfers on the SEPA Credit Transfer (SCT) and SEPA Instant Credit Transfer (SCT Inst) rails. The process runs from instruction capture — via online and mobile banking, the corporate host-to-host file channel, and branch — through format validation, funds checking, multi-layer compliance screening, rail routing, debit booking, clearing submission, settlement confirmation, and end-of-day reconciliation. It also covers the handling of R-transactions — returns, rejects, recalls and refunds — and applies to both single payments and bulk file-based corporate batches. This process is the operational backbone of the bank's euro payment offering and sits squarely in scope of the SEPA scheme rulebooks, PSD2, AML and CFT obligations, and sanctions regimes.

parts.heading: Current state
parts.text:
The process is documented across six of the seven As-Is sections — overview, roles, process steps, exceptions, metrics and process gaps — with the pain-points section containing no elements at all.

The overview records the process owner (Head of Payment Operations), scope boundaries, trigger conditions, inputs and outputs, and confirms the process as an As-Is draft approved in May 2026. The scope boundary is clean: outbound SCT and SCT Inst only, with inbound SEPA payments, direct debits, non-euro cross-border payments and card payments all explicitly excluded.

Five roles are documented. The Customer (ROLE-SPP-001) initiates the payment across three channels and re-engages only when a validation rejection or fraud step-up demands it. Payments Operations (ROLE-SPP-002) is the operational centre of gravity — responsible for validation, funds checking, clearing submission, customer confirmation, end-of-day reconciliation and R-transaction handling, and consulted on screening decisions. The Ops Approver (ROLE-SPP-003) provides a mandatory four-eyes review before any bulk payment file is released. Compliance (ROLE-SPP-004) owns sanctions and AML screening, investigating confirmed hits and, with Financial Crime, blocking release pending the outcome. Fraud (ROLE-SPP-005) is accountable for real-time fraud scoring and the release-or-cancel decision after step-up verification. Together the five roles cover the process cleanly, with defined accountability at every decision point.

Ten process steps document the end-to-end flow in a logical, connected sequence. Instruction capture (PS-SPP-001) receives pain.001 messages for bulk files and structured records for single payments from all three channels. Validation (PS-SPP-002) checks six conditions — IBAN structure, scheme-directory reachability, EUR currency, SEPA zone membership, mandatory fields, and beneficiary name via Verification of Payee — and also runs a 24-hour duplicate check; any failure routes to EX-SPP-001. The funds check (PS-SPP-003) confirms available balance including intraday credit limits and earmarks the amount; it re-runs on any payment released from a downstream sanctions or fraud hold before it continues. Sanctions and AML screening (PS-SPP-004) runs before the debit posts; false positives loop back to funds check, while confirmed hits freeze the payment under EX-SPP-003. Real-time fraud scoring (PS-SPP-005) passes low-risk items automatically and holds high-risk ones for step-up verification under EX-SPP-004; a released item loops back to funds check before continuing to routing. The routing decision (PS-SPP-006) selects the rail: SCT Inst when the amount is at or below the bank-set EUR 100,000 limit, the customer elected instant, and the creditor is instant-reachable; otherwise standard SCT applies, with a 16:00 CET same-day cut-off. Debit booking (PS-SPP-007) converts the earmark to a posted debit before clearing submission — the point of financial exposure from which a non-settling payment must be unwound. Clearing submission (PS-SPP-008) generates a pacs.008 message and sends it to the appropriate CSM gateway: RT1 for instant payments, STEP2 for standard. Settlement and confirmation (PS-SPP-009) receives the outcome and sends an automated hub notification to the customer — immediately for SCT Inst (within the 10-second scheme clock from submission to RT1) and on STEP2-cycle confirmation for standard SCT. End-of-day reconciliation (PS-SPP-010) closes the cycle: Payments Operations compares every submitted payment against the CSM settlement reports, investigates any break the same day, and triggers a debit reversal for any confirmed non-settlement.

Six exceptions are documented. EX-SPP-001 (Invalid IBAN/BIC or incomplete instruction, MEDIUM impact) is the process's front-line rejection: any of the six validation checks failing routes here and requires the customer to correct and resubmit. EX-SPP-002 (Insufficient funds, LOW impact) covers both first-pass failures and the more nuanced case of a looped-back payment that fails a fresh funds check after a downstream hold — single items are rejected, while bulk items requeue for up to three cycles before rejection. EX-SPP-003 (Confirmed sanctions/AML hit, HIGH impact) results in cancellation and regulatory reporting; because screening precedes debit booking, no reversal is required. EX-SPP-004 (High fraud score, MEDIUM impact) holds the payment pending customer step-up verification, then releases or cancels depending on the outcome. EX-SPP-005 (SCT Inst timeout or beneficiary-bank rejection, LOW impact) falls back to standard SCT where routing rules still permit it, or reverses the debit and re-credits the customer otherwise. EX-SPP-007 (Missed cut-off, LOW impact) rolls a standard SCT to the next cycle or next business day. EX-SPP-006 is absent from the wiki, though it sits within the exception-numbering sequence, suggesting an element that was removed or never completed.

The metrics section defines six performance targets: a 10-second SCT Inst end-to-end time (M-SPP-001), same-day execution before the cut-off and D+1 after it (M-SPP-002 and M-SPP-003), exception resolution within one business day of identification (M-SPP-004), recall and return actioning within the 10-business-day EPC deadline (M-SPP-005), and the 16:00 CET standard-SCT cut-off threshold (M-SPP-006). Not one of these carries a measured actual; every metric records "not measured" in its value field.

Six process gaps are open, all from source-document provenance and requiring SME follow-up. PG-SPP-001 asks whether the EUR 100,000 SCT Inst threshold at the routing decision is a bank-set limit or the SEPA scheme maximum — a question that determines whether room exists to raise the instant ceiling. PG-SPP-002 and PG-SPP-003 concern bulk files: how a file mixing instant-eligible and standard items is routed today is undocumented (PG-SPP-002), and the approval path for files that exceed a corporate daily aggregate limit is unconfirmed (PG-SPP-003). PG-SPP-004 queries whether SCT Inst is available 24/7 including weekends and public holidays. PG-SPP-005 notes that the handling of Verification of Payee results — whether a name mismatch blocks the payment outright or only warns — is not documented despite the check appearing explicitly in the validation step. PG-SPP-006, the most consequential gap, records that bank-initiated outbound recalls are in scope under R-transactions but documented nowhere: the trigger, owning role, system, and scheme deadline are all unknown.

parts.heading: What stands out
parts.text:
The process documentation is substantively coherent. The ten-step sequence is logically connected and the loop-back mechanics — released holds returning to the funds check, sanctions false positives looping back before continuing to fraud screening — are explicitly captured, which is uncommon at this stage of process documentation and reflects careful SME engagement. The decision to run sanctions and AML screening before the debit posts is correctly noted as the architectural choice that determines whether EX-SPP-003 requires a reversal; it does not. Five roles carry clear accountabilities without material overlap, and the exception catalogue addresses both high-severity, low-frequency events (EX-SPP-003) and everyday nuisances (EX-SPP-001). The process boundary is sharply drawn.

The most consequential weakness is the complete absence of a pain-points section: it contains no elements. The As-Is picture therefore carries no signal from the people who run this process every day — no friction, no rework, no manual workaround, no recurring delay is captured. That gap leaves the target-state design without the operational evidence it most needs to make justified redesign choices.

The metrics section is structurally present but substantively hollow. All six metrics carry only scheme targets and no measured actuals. The bank is documenting what the process should do rather than what it is doing, which means any gap analysis against a future-state target will rest on assumed rather than observed performance. Beyond the metrics, PG-SPP-005 and PG-SPP-006 are not incremental questions: one asks how the bank handles a check that already appears in the validation step, and the other records that an entire in-scope process path — outbound recalls — has no documented handling at all.

Eleven elements carry an approval status of "in-progress" rather than "approved," including the central Payments Operations role (ROLE-SPP-002), the first two steps (PS-SPP-001 and PS-SPP-002), the debit-booking and clearing-submission steps (PS-SPP-007 and PS-SPP-008), and four of the six exceptions. These have passed a lint review but have not received formal SME sign-off, so a meaningful portion of the core process flow remains provisionally documented.

parts.heading: Recommendation
parts.text: Three things should happen before this area can serve as a reliable foundation for target-state design. First, elicit and document the pain-points section: without it, the innovation and transformation areas will be designed against an incomplete picture of what the process actually costs to run operationally, and any improvement case will be weaker for the absence. Second, close the six open process gaps, prioritising PG-SPP-005 (what the bank does with a Verification of Payee name mismatch today) and PG-SPP-006 (the complete outbound-recall path), both of which leave substantive in-scope process behaviour entirely undocumented. Third, replace the "not measured" value fields across all six metrics with observed actuals: the target state cannot be designed credibly, and the transformation cannot be justified commercially, without knowing where the process stands today against its own SLAs. Completing these three steps, alongside clearing the eleven in-progress elements to full SME approval, would bring the As-Is area to a state fit for the Risk and Compliance and Target Process specialists to work from.

parts.heading: Introduction
parts.text: The Risk & Compliance area covers four sections for the SEPA Payment Processing process (SPP): Controls, Regulation, Control Gaps, and Audit Findings. SEPA Payment Processing handles outbound euro credit transfers on the standard SCT and instant SCT Inst rails, managing payments from corporate and retail customers through validation, clearing, settlement and reconciliation. It operates under strict EPC scheme rules, anti-money-laundering obligations and sanctions regimes, which means the health of its compliance framework directly determines the bank's regulatory exposure and its continued eligibility as a SEPA scheme participant. The Risk & Compliance area is therefore one of the most consequential sections of this process wiki.

parts.heading: Current state
parts.text:
Of the four sections that make up the Risk & Compliance area, only Controls has been documented. Eight controls are recorded — CP-SPP-001 through CP-SPP-008 — spanning the validate-instruction, funds-check, screening, fraud-scoring, bulk-file release, instant-SLA monitoring and end-of-day reconciliation steps. Five of the eight are preventive and three are detective; six are fully automated and two are manual, with ownership split across Payments Operations (six controls), Compliance (one) and Fraud (one).

Together the eight controls trace a coherent chain. CP-SPP-001 (IBAN/BIC validation against the scheme directory) and CP-SPP-002 (duplicate-payment detection with a 24-hour rolling window) gate every instruction at the validate-instruction step, rejecting malformed routing data and inadvertent re-submissions before they advance. CP-SPP-003 (funds and limit check) earmarks the payment amount before debit booking, preventing unauthorised overdrafts and settlement risk. CP-SPP-004 (sanctions and AML screening, owned by Compliance) screens both debtor and creditor against sanctions lists and subjects the payment to AML transaction monitoring, with potential hits routed to Compliance before the item reaches clearing. CP-SPP-005 (real-time fraud scoring, owned by Fraud) scores every payment as it arrives, holding high-risk items automatically, so fraudulent payments cannot settle before human review. The two manual controls complement the automated layer: CP-SPP-006 (4-eyes release of bulk payment files) ensures an Ops Approver independently reviews and releases each bulk file before submission, preventing an erroneous or unauthorised batch from reaching clearing without challenge; and CP-SPP-007 (end-of-day reconciliation against CSM settlement reports) detects any break between what the bank submitted and what the clearing mechanism settled. CP-SPP-008 (SCT Inst SLA monitoring) closes the loop on scheme compliance for instant payments by continuously and automatically tracking adherence to the 10-second end-to-end settlement rule.

The Regulation section contains no elements. Given that SEPA processing is directly governed by the EPC SCT and SCT Inst Rulebooks, the EU Funds Transfer Regulation, AML Directives and applicable sanctions regimes, this is a significant blank in the wiki's compliance picture. The Control Gaps and Audit Findings sections are likewise empty, with no gap or finding elements recorded.

parts.heading: What stands out
parts.text:
The control set is strong where it matters most: six of the eight controls are fully automated, covering every payment item on the critical path from instruction receipt through validation, sanctions screening and fraud scoring. The 4-eyes requirement on bulk files is the right manual compensating control in a domain where a single erroneous submission can affect thousands of payments simultaneously. The end-of-day reconciliation, though manual, provides a sound daily backstop that would catch any settlement discrepancy that slipped through inline.

The provenance picture is less clean. Six of the eight controls — CP-SPP-003 through CP-SPP-008 — carry a "proposed" provenance tag on their "Risk addressed" section, meaning the risk narrative in each case was AI-proposed and has not yet been confirmed by the subject-matter expert. CP-SPP-001 and CP-SPP-002 are the only controls with fully elicited or document-evidenced risk statements. This does not undermine the substance of the controls, which are drawn from source documentation, but it does mean none of the six can be approved under the wiki's approval-gating rules.

The more material issue is the complete absence of the Regulation, Control Gaps and Audit Findings sections. Without a regulation section the wiki cannot demonstrate that the eight documented controls collectively satisfy the process's regulatory obligations, nor identify which obligations are as yet uncovered. Without control gaps, no known weaknesses are on record. Without audit findings, there is no visibility into what internal or external audit has previously raised and whether those issues have been remediated.

parts.heading: Recommendation
parts.text: The immediate priority is to populate the Regulation section. The EPC SCT and SCT Inst Rulebooks, the EU Funds Transfer Regulation, AML Directive obligations and applicable sanctions regimes should each be documented as regulation elements, with their controls cross-referenced so the wiki can confirm what each obligation is met by. Once the regulatory frame exists, a Control Gaps pass should compare the eight documented controls against those requirements, surfacing any mandatory control that is absent. Audit Findings should be added as soon as the latest audit cycle's output is available. In parallel, the six controls with "proposed" risk-addressed provenance — CP-SPP-003 through CP-SPP-008 — should be reviewed with the subject-matter expert to confirm that the documented risk narrative is accurate, enabling the controls to advance from draft to approved.
